mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 318is engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 318is engine typically costs £1,500–£2,800 supply only depending on generation. Fitting adds £650–£1,300. Used M42 engines can be found for sub-£200, while a full rebuild with timing chain work costs around £1,475–£2,200. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

Yes — the M42 and M44 are widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable four-cylinder engines. Many owners report 200,000+ miles with proper maintenance. However, the M42 has a well-documented profile gasket weakness, and cooling system components are a known weak point on all 318is engines. Check the Era Map above for your specific engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 318is?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main known issues are: (1) Timing case profile gasket failure on the M42 — can cause rapid oil loss and engine failure, (2) Cooling system failure — water pump, radiator, expansion tank and hoses, (3) Timing chain tensioner wear — causing cold-start rattle, and (4) DISA valve failure on the M44 — causing loss of low-end torque and rattling.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 318is engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £3,000–£8,000 318is (E30 values are significantly higher), a £2,200 recon is 25–75% of the car's value. For a clean E30 example, replacement is almost always worth it given rising classic values. For an E36 in average condition, evaluate the car's overall condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 318is engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: E30 and early E36 (1989–1995) have the M42B18 (1.8L); late E36 (1996–1999) have the M44B19 (1.9L). The M42 has a large vane-type air flow meter; the M44 has a MAF sensor and DISA on the inlet manifold. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M42 profile gasket, or does it need engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the profile gasket can be replaced without removing the engine. The repair typically costs £500–£1,000 and is far cheaper than the £2,500+ engine replacement that follows if the gasket fails and oil loss causes engine seizure. BMW updated the gasket material to address the issue. If you have an M42, having the profile gasket replaced preventatively is strongly recommended.

eraMap.title: BMW 318is — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 1.8L 16v DOHC — the original twin-cam four. Rev-happy, smooth and famously durable
Same 1.8L M42 — Bosch Motronic 1.7. Profile gasket is the main weak point
1.9L M44 — DISA variable intake, OBD2. Smoother, more refined, same core reliability

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 318is engine typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

cta.label: → Compare 318is engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Case Profile Gasket Failure (M42, E30 & early E36)
problems.symptoms: Oil leak from the front of the engine, oil dripping onto the ground, low oil level, potential engine seizure if ignored

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you notice oil leaks

problems.recommendation: The timing case profile gasket is the most notorious weak point on the M42 engine. The profile gasket seals the lower cam chain case to the underside of the cylinder head. BMW updated the gasket material and instituted a warranty program, but many cars have not had this addressed. If the gasket fails, oil can leak out rapidly, leading to catastrophic engine failure. One owner reported: "the timing case profile gasket let go twice, which cooked two engines". If you see oil leaks from the front of the engine, book an inspection immediately. Replacing the gasket is far cheaper than replacing the engine.

failureLink.label: Read the full profile gasket failure guide → /failures/m42-profile-gasket/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Cooling System Failure (All Engines)
problems.symptoms: Overheating, coolant loss, temperature gauge rising, steam from engine bay, heater blowing cold
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: overheating can cause catastrophic engine damage

problems.recommendation: The cooling system on the 318is is a well-known weak point. As one specialist noted: "if it's got 100k on it, the water pump, radiator, and expansion tank should have been replaced already or else they need to be done". The factory water pump had a plastic impeller that can fail — replacing it with a metal impeller version is a common upgrade. Common failures include the radiator end tanks cracking, plastic coolant pipes under the intake manifold, and the thermostat sticking. Never let the engine go near the red zone on the temperature gauge — shut it down and get it towed.

failureLink.label: Read the full cooling system failure guide

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.symptoms: Rattling or clanking noise on cold start, ticking from the front of the engine, metallic noise that subsides as oil pressure builds
problems.replacementCost: £2,500–£4,000 (if chain jumps or breaks — full engine replacement)
problems.recommendation: The M42 and M44 have a timing chain that can suffer from tensioner wear. One owner reported: "the rattling you've been hearing was probably the tensioner going bad. They have a tendency to do that". If the tensioner cannot keep the chain tight, the chain can jump time. Since the M42 and M44 are interference engines, a broken timing chain will cause pistons to hit valves, requiring a full engine replacement. At the first sign of cold-start rattling, have the tensioner inspected. Replacing the tensioner is a relatively inexpensive preventative measure.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain tensioner guide

problems.title: DISA Valve Failure (M44, 1996–1999)
problems.symptoms: Loss of low-end torque, rough idle, hesitation on acceleration, rattling noise from the intake manifold
problems.recommendation: The M44 engine introduced BMW's DISA (Differentiated Intake System) — a variable intake manifold system that optimises torque across the rev range. The DISA valve has a plastic flap that can wear or break over time, causing a rattling noise and loss of low-end torque. A broken DISA flap can also send debris into the engine. If you experience poor low-end response or hear a rattle from the intake, have the DISA valve inspected. Rebuild kits are available, or the entire unit can be replaced.

failureLink.label: Read the full DISA valve failure guide → /failures/disa-valve-failure/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches the fault before oil loss causes engine failure
Preventative replacement is recommended at 60k–100k miles
Interference engine — piston-valve contact means full rebuild or replacement

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 318is? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented profile gasket replacement (M42 engines) — this is the critical E30/E36 early issue
* Cooling system overhaul completed (water pump with metal impeller, radiator, hoses, thermostat)
* Full service history with regular oil changes
* No timing chain rattle on cold start
avoidIf: * Oil leaks from the front of the engine with no documented profile gasket work
* Overheating history or cooling system issues — the M42/M44 is unforgiving of overheating
* Cold-start rattling that hasn't been diagnosed
* Poor service history — these engines are reliable but need proper maintenance

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 318is is one of BMW's most underrated drivers' cars — a 16-valve twin-cam four-cylinder that offers 6-cylinder performance with 4-cylinder economy. The M42 and M44 are famously durable engines, with many owners reporting 200,000+ miles. But they have well-documented weak points: the profile gasket on the M42, the cooling system on all engines, and the timing chain tensioner. Buy with documented maintenance history, particularly profile gasket and cooling system work, and you'll have a genuinely rewarding classic that will keep on giving.

cta.label: → Compare 318is engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 318is (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Profile Gasket — Has the timing case profile gasket been replaced? (M42 engines — critical)
* Cooling System — Check for coolant leaks; ask about water pump (metal impeller?), radiator, thermostat history
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clanking from the front of the engine (timing chain tensioner)
* Oil Leaks — Look for oil residue around the front of the engine and timing case
* DISA Valve — Any rattling from the intake or loss of low-end torque? (M44 engines)
* Service History — Including which engine (M42 or M44) and confirmed oil change intervals
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
